The first phase needs ten hours while the second needs twenty.
Phase three requires forty hours and phase four needs eighty.
The final stage takes one hundred sixty hours to finish.
These five sums add up to three hundred ten total.
Now the time for each part grows by one and a half times.
If the original plan was twenty weeks then the new time is thirty.
This shift changes the schedule but not the order of work.
Teams must adapt to the longer duration for every step.
No delays in sequence will occur despite the added time.
The manager will track progress closely to ensure success.
Resources may need adjustment to handle the extra effort.
Communication between groups must remain clear and frequent.
Fortune favors those who plan well and stay flexible.
The project will finish later than first expected by all.
Everyone should focus on quality during this extended period.
Strict timelines help keep the work organized and on track.
Coordination remains key to overcoming these new hurdles.
